title: AI Trust & Safety Digest
tagline: A curated current snapshot of AI trust, safety, and governance, including as many materially important fresh items as the day warrants.
lead: Today’s signal is concentrated in practical governance and assurance for deployed AI systems: auditing whether models truly forget data, bounding privacy leakage in agent workflows, and adding runtime controls for production agents. A secondary thread is transparency and standards, with new proposals on tracing hidden model dependencies and support for EU content provenance rules.

items.title: New framework for auditing machine unlearning
items.why_it_matters: Improves verification that deletion requests are actually honored, strengthening privacy and compliance claims.

items.title: OCELOT proposes inference-leakage budgets for privacy-preserving LLM agents
items.why_it_matters: Treats privacy as an end-to-end agent property, not a single-output filter.

items.title: A Five-Plane Reference Architecture for Runtime Governance of Production AI Agents
items.why_it_matters: Offers a concrete control architecture for supervising agent actions across tools, data, and execution.

items.title: ALIGNBEAM introduces inference-time alignment transfer across model vocabularies
items.why_it_matters: Could preserve safety behavior when domain fine-tuning weakens refusal and safeguard performance.

items.title: Anatomy of Post-Training uses interpretability to inspect how data shapes model behavior
items.why_it_matters: Gives developers more visibility into which post-training signals produce desired or spurious behaviors.

items.title: OpenAI backs Europe’s trustworthy AI ecosystem work on content transparency
items.why_it_matters: Signals support for provenance standards and EU code-of-practice mechanisms around AI-generated content.

items.title: Which Models Are Our Models Built On? Auditing Invisible Dependencies in Modern LLMs
items.why_it_matters: Targets supply-chain transparency for model development, including hidden upstream model dependencies.

items.title: Should LLM Agents Decide in Social Simulations? Comparing Finite-State and LLM-Based Decision Policies
items.why_it_matters: Highlights methodological risks when opaque LLM decisions distort research or policy simulations.

note: Selected from recent trust-and-safety relevant posts and papers by labs, evaluators, standards bodies, policy organizations, and arXiv, then summarized with GPT-5.4. Includes as many materially important fresh items as the day warrants.
